content.assessment: Shrinking the state-sector selective footprint reduces the absolute scale of early tracking, which evidence associates with inequality (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01), but contraction without admission-mechanism reform risks concentrating remaining scarce places even more intensely among already-advantaged families who currently show disproportionate access even controlling for ability (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02). The proposal's own risk analysis anticipates displacement to church/foundation schools, which is corroborated by documented growth in that segment and its association with segregation amplification (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07).
opportunities: Could be paired with SES-aware admission redesign (as in TP-live-t1) to prevent concentration-of-advantage side effect
Reducing state-sector track volume could, if church-school absorption is checked, reduce net system-wide early-tracking exposure
strengths: Reduces the overall scale of the early-tracking mechanism shown to be associated with system-wide inequality (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01)
Uses demographic decline as cover, potentially reducing political resistance to contraction relative to overt equity-motivated reform
threats: Documented rise in church-school share (8.6% to 16.6% primary; 10% to 26.1% secondary between 2010-2021) suggests displaced demand could migrate to a sector outside contraction jurisdiction, undermining net equity effect and potentially worsening segregation via privatization-driven amplification (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07)
Geographic disparities in remaining track access could worsen if contraction is not geographically balanced, compounding existing rural-urban disparities (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08)
weaknesses: Contraction without addressing the admission mechanism could concentrate remaining places further among SES-advantaged families already shown to have disproportionate access (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02)
Does not address Roma segregation, a distinct and worsening mechanism independent of overall selective-track size (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-06)
Does not address primary-school-choice or geographic-access sorting mechanisms operating independently of state-sector capacity (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08)
